description: The largest North American oak and a foothill-to-valley keystone: hundreds of native caterpillar species convert its foliage into bird food, while acorns and cavities structure food webs for mammals, woodpeckers, and jays. In Tallamy terms, oaks are foundational hubs because so many specialist herbivores and frugivores are oak-obligate or oak-strongly associated.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Deep soils on gentle to moderate slopes; tolerates seasonal flooding in deeper pockets; avoid thin, baking ridgetops unless soil is deep enough
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Deep loam to clay; tolerates heavy clay where drainage is not stagnant; pH adaptable

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental summer irrigation once established; frequent summer watering near the trunk increases Phytophthora root-rot risk. Watering only the first 2–3 years, in cool months, if needed.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove deadwood, broken branches, and minor crown cleaning
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune during full winter dormancy. Avoid warm-season pruning wounds that can attract oak pests and complicate disease management.
maintenance.specialNotes: Maintain a mulch-free collar 12–24 inches from the trunk; do not irrigate within the drip line of established trees.

seed.description: Acorns mature in fall; a staple for jays, woodpeckers, squirrels, and deer, and a seed bank for stand regeneration

phenology.ecologicalValue: Exceptional caterpillar diversity supports nesting songbirds; acorns drive autumn-winter energy budgets; bark and cavities provide nesting and roosting structure.

wildlife.notes: Large silk-moth larvae commonly feed on oak foliage; oak-rich neighborhoods export moth biomass to bats and birds.
Oak-feeding nymphalid whose caterpillars tie leaves with silk; part of the oak-specialist guild that fuels insectivorous birds.
Colonial cavity nester that centers territories on oaks for acorn storage and granary trees.
Forages on oak bark for insects year-round; uses oak woodlands for cover and nesting substrate.

description: A drought-deciduous foothill oak that anchors blue-oak woodland food webs with massive insect productivity and reliable mast in mast years. Tallamy-style habitat gains come from its long-lived canopy and the specialist herbivores that treat oak foliage as primary food.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Best on dry, well-drained slopes and foothill benches; avoid cold-air pockets and poorly drained bottoms
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Tolerant of many mineral soils if drainage is fast; pH roughly 6.0–8.0; rocky foothill soils are typical

maintenance.wateringNotes: Calscape lists very low water and caps summer irrigation for established trees (at most about once monthly if truly needed). Default is none—especially near the trunk.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove deadwood and structurally weak branches
maintenance.pruningNotes: Winter pruning only; minimize crown wounding during warm months.
maintenance.specialNotes: Slow-growing; plan for decades-scale development; protect root zone from grade changes and summer lawn irrigation.

seed.description: Acorns ripen late summer into fall; important seasonal food where crops are large enough to attract granivores and scatter-hoarders

phenology.ecologicalValue: High caterpillar loads support breeding birds; acorns support jays, woodpeckers, and mammals; canopy shade moderates understory microclimates.

wildlife.notes: Specialist oak defoliator whose larvae feed on blue oak foliage; part of the oak insect guild that supports insectivorous birds.
Adults nectar widely; oaks contribute landscape structure used for movement and thermal cover near nectar sources.
Stores acorns when mast is available; tightly linked to oak woodlands for winter calories.
Cavity nester of oak woodlands that forages on insects gleaned from oak bark and foliage.

description: Evergreen foothill oak that bridges chaparral and oak woodland, sustaining a dense canopy of insect herbivory year-round. It functions like other western oaks as a community-scale food factory for insectivorous birds and small mammals.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Common on foothill slopes and rocky sites; tolerates thin soils better than valley oak if drainage is adequate
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained rocky to loamy soils; drought-tolerant once established

maintenance.wateringNotes: Avoid regular summer irrigation after establishment; keep supplemental water rare, localized, and away from the trunk.
maintenance.pruningTask: Deadwood removal and light crown cleaning
maintenance.pruningNotes: Dormant-season pruning reduces stress and disease-entry risks compared with summer cuts.
maintenance.specialNotes: Evergreen foliage supports winter-active insectivores when deciduous neighbors are bare.

seed.description: Acorns mature in autumn; consumed by birds and mammals and cached for dispersal

phenology.ecologicalValue: Year-round canopy fuels caterpillars and other insects; acorns extend autumn mast; dense foliage provides thermal cover.

wildlife.notes: Often nectars in oak woodland edges; landscape connectivity increases use near foothill gardens.
Oak-specialist caterpillars feed on new foliage; a classic interior live oak herbivore in foothill woodlands.
Uses live oaks for granaries and nesting where acorn crops and suitable snags or limbs exist.
Forages for insects and tender plant material in oak woodlands during the breeding season.

description: A dramatic early-season nectar fountain in foothill landscapes, but not a classical keystone like oaks because its seasonal leaf-drop and chemistry narrow the guilds it supports. Still valuable in Tallamy framing as a pulse resource that feeds diverse native pollinators early, while honey bees can be harmed by toxic nectar compounds that many native bees tolerate better.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Naturally on dry slopes and canyon bottoms; avoid soggy soils
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Tolerates a range of soils with decent drainage; drought-deciduous strategy means summer soils should dry down

maintenance.wateringNotes: Respect its summer dormancy: little to no irrigation when leafless. Overwatering in summer can favor pathogens and weaken the natural cycle.
maintenance.pruningTask: Shape young trees; remove dead twigs
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune in winter dormancy; minimize heavy summer pruning on stressed trees.
maintenance.specialNotes: Nectar contains compounds toxic to western honey bees; many native bees are comparatively resistant—site away from managed hives if that is a concern.

seed.description: Large shiny seeds mature late summer into fall; important for some rodents when handled carefully due to toxicity if eaten raw

phenology.ecologicalValue: Early-season nectar and pollen for native bees and hummingbirds; seeds for scatter-hoarding rodents where safe; summer bare branches still provide perch structure.

wildlife.notes: Visits buckeye inflorescences during the big spring bloom pulse in foothill gardens.
Common native bumble bee on buckeye flowers; native bees are generally less impacted by buckeye nectar toxicity than managed honey bees.
A widespread hawkmoth that nectars at night and dusk; buckeye adds early-season volume to moth energy budgets.
Caches large seeds when available; movement links individual trees to broader landscape seed dynamics.

description: Evergreen chaparral and woodland shrub whose winter berry crops stitch together bird food webs when many other fruits are gone. In Tallamy terms it is a keystone fruiting shrub because its berries are accessible, abundant, and timed to support migratory and resident frugivores.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on slopes; tolerates chaparral exposure once established
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained soils typical of foothills; tolerates drought and lean mineral soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: No regular summer water once established; occasional deep soak the first year only if planting in extreme heat.
maintenance.pruningTask: Thin for air flow; reduce crowding on structures
maintenance.pruningNotes: Winter/early spring shaping preserves summer berries and reduces stress.
maintenance.specialNotes: Leave fruiting wood if the goal is wildlife; heavy shearing removes winter food.

berry.description: Bright red pomes peak in winter, bridging the hungry season for thrushes, waxwings, and other fruit specialists

phenology.ecologicalValue: Winter fruit pulse; dense evergreen cover; insect substrate on leaves and bark supports insectivores.

wildlife.notes: Wintering thrushes rely heavily on persistent native fruits like toyon in foothill woodlands.
Forms flocks on toyon when crops are heavy; classic winter spectacle in California.
Uses small white flowers during summer bloom.
Defends fruiting shrubs; toyon increases winter carrying capacity in residential edges.

description: A nitrogen-fixing chaparral shrub whose spring nectar blitz feeds early pollinators and supports a distinctive set of insect herbivores. Tallamy-style value comes from tight coupling between early-season resources and pollinator population build-up before summer dryness.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Best on slopes with excellent air drainage; classic chaparral exposure
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Fast-draining, lean soils; intolerant of prolonged summer wet around the crown

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer irrigation once established; first-year watering should be infrequent and deep, tapering off quickly.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead interior wood after establishment; avoid heavy shearing
maintenance.pruningNotes: If shaping is needed, do minimal cuts in cool months; over-pruning removes flower buds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Root disturbance during planting is common—prefer small containers and avoid circling roots.

berry.description: Dry capsules mature in early summer; small seeds for granivores and soil seed banks

seed.description: Capsules release small seeds after bloom; heat and smoke can cue germination in natural systems

phenology.ecologicalValue: Spring nectar hotspot; foliage supports specialist herbivores; seeds extend early-summer food for small birds and rodents.

wildlife.notes: Heavy use of Ceanothus inflorescences during the spring nectar peak.
Nectars on early-season shrubs; Ceanothus is part of the foothill spring corridor.
Night-flying hawkmoth that tracks big floral displays in warm foothill nights.
Feeds on small seeds and uses shrubby structure for cover near food plants.

description: A foothill manzanita whose urn-shaped winter-spring flowers feed early pollinators while evergreen foliage supports insect gleaning for birds. It behaves as a chaparral keystone shrub by extending forage and cover through the dry season.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Slopes and ridges with excellent drainage; intolerant of basin plantings with summer moisture
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, acidic to circumneutral rocky soils typical of Sierra foothill chaparral

maintenance.wateringNotes: Establish with occasional deep water in cool months only; avoid sprinklers hitting foliage in summer.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead wood; lightly thin if needed for structure
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal pruning after bloom if you want maximum fruit for wildlife; avoid heavy shearing.
maintenance.specialNotes: Do not amend planting holes with rich compost that holds summer moisture against roots.

berry.description: Small mealy berries (madrone bears) feed birds and mammals in summer

phenology.ecologicalValue: Early nectar for bees, hummingbirds, and spring butterflies; summer fruit for birds; evergreen cover for chaparral birds.

wildlife.notes: Uses manzanita flowers during winter-spring when hummingbird territories expand.
Early-season nymphalid that uses manzanita and other chaparral flowers during late-winter nectar gaps.
Skulking chaparral endemic that uses dense shrubs for cover and forages on small fruits.
Buzz-pollinates urn-shaped flowers where vibration releases pollen.

description: Evergreen shrub that anchors disturbed foothill slopes and woodland edges. A keystone genus in Tallamy's framework — its late-season bloom is one of the last major nectar sources of the year, fueling migrating butterflies and overwintering native bees. Dense branching provides year-round bird cover.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on slopes and disturbed ground; a pioneer species that stabilizes erosion-prone sites
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Tolerates a wide range of soils from heavy clay to sandy loam; drought-tolerant once established

maintenance.wateringNotes: No irrigation needed after establishment. Extremely drought-tolerant — one of the toughest native shrubs for hot, dry foothill gardens.
maintenance.pruningTask: Shape in late winter; can be hedged hard or left natural; remove dead interior wood to improve air flow
maintenance.pruningNotes: Responds well to hard pruning. Female plants produce cottony seed dispersal — choose male cultivar 'Twin Peaks' if desired.
maintenance.specialNotes: Dioecious — plant both male and female if possible to support the full pollinator guild. The prostrate form ('Pigeon Point') works as groundcover.

seed.description: Female plants produce cottony achenes that disperse by wind through winter

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical late-season nectar when almost nothing else blooms — bridges the autumn gap for migrating and overwintering pollinators.

wildlife.notes: One of the last major nectar sources for fall migrants; Painted Ladies congregate on coyote brush during southbound movement.
Queens forage heavily on late-blooming coyote brush before entering winter dormancy.
Flocks roost and build pendant nests in dense coyote brush canopy year-round.
Buckeye butterfly caterpillars feed on Baccharis foliage in foothill and coastal habitats.

description: Spectacular multi-stemmed large shrub or small tree whose magenta-pink flowers erupt on bare branches in early spring — one of the first and most important pollinator resources in Sierra foothill oak woodland. Heart-shaped leaves turn gold in fall. Flat seed pods persist through winter, providing food for birds. A signature plant of the Auburn foothills with 94 local observations.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Thrives on dry, rocky slopes; excellent planted on hillsides beneath oaks or at woodland margins
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained soils; tolerates heavy clay to rocky decomposed granite; drought-tolerant once established

maintenance.wateringNotes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Occasional deep summer water in the first 2-3 years helps root development; after that, natural rainfall is sufficient in most foothill gardens.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove crossing branches and suckers to shape; thin interior for air circulation
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune after bloom in late spring/early summer. Can be trained as a single-trunk small tree or left as a multi-stemmed shrub. Avoid heavy pruning — redbud compartmentalizes wounds slowly.
maintenance.specialNotes: Nitrogen-fixing legume — improves soil for neighboring plants. Seeds require scarification for germination in the wild.

seed.description: Flat reddish-brown seed pods persist on branches through fall and winter, providing food for birds

phenology.ecologicalValue: One of the earliest and most important spring nectar sources for native bees and butterflies when the foothill landscape is still mostly dormant. Seed pods extend wildlife value through fall and winter.

wildlife.notes: Early spring blooms are a critical nectar source for territorial hummingbirds just as breeding season begins.
Early-emerging queens depend on redbud flowers when few other nectar sources are available in the foothills.
Callophrys henrici larvae feed on redbud flowers and developing seed pods in spring.
Picks seeds from persistent pods through fall and winter in oak-woodland understory.

description: A long-blooming buckwheat whose flat-topped inflorescences are pollinator magnets across the dry season, linking early bees to late-season butterflies. In Tallamy framing, buckwheats are keystone forage plants because they provide sustained nectar and pollen when many shrubs have finished flowering.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on well-drained slopes and rocky openings; intolerant of heavy clay that stays wet
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, fast-draining soils; typical of serpentine and rocky foothill openings

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer irrigation once established; overwatering shortens life in wild-type plantings.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove spent flowering stems after seeds drop if tidiness is desired
maintenance.pruningNotes: Late fall cleanup preserves seeds for granivores; skip if you want maximum self-sowing.
maintenance.specialNotes: Flower color varies among populations; expect cream to yellow forms common in foothill gardens.

seed.description: Small achenes mature in fall; valuable to small seed-eating birds and for local regeneration

phenology.ecologicalValue: Long-season nectar and pollen; host relationships for blues and hairstreaks; seeds for finches.

wildlife.notes: Buckwheats are classic host plants for blues in California; supports local population persistence.
Tracks summer buckwheat blooms during warm-season nectar gaps.
Common generalist at small flat-topped inflorescences in sunny openings.
Works buckwheat heads through the dry season when many garden exotics are not useful.

description: Low, spreading subshrub whose brilliant scarlet-orange tubular flowers erupt from late summer through fall — exactly when most foothill natives have finished blooming. This makes it the single most important late-season hummingbird resource in Sierra foothill gardens, bridging the nectar gap between spring bloomers and winter dormancy.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Rocky slopes, road cuts, dry banks; excellent on south-facing exposures
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy or rocky soil; tolerates poor, dry soils; avoid heavy clay or wet winter conditions

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer irrigation once established — it blooms during drought on stored root moisture. Occasional deep water in first year only.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut back to a few inches in late winter before new growth emerges
maintenance.pruningNotes: Hard annual cutback keeps plants compact and floriferous; skip this and plants become leggy and woody.
maintenance.specialNotes: Spreads by underground rhizomes — give it room or use a root barrier near paths. Gray-leaved forms tolerate more heat.

seed.description: Small wind-dispersed seeds with cottony hairs mature as flowers fade; moderate self-sowing in open ground

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical late-season nectar source when almost nothing else blooms; bridges the summer–fall gap for hummingbirds and native bees preparing for winter.

wildlife.notes: Primary late-season nectar source; hummingbirds defend individual plants as feeding territories.
Hawkmoths visit the tubular red flowers at dusk, providing nocturnal pollination services.
Workers and queens forage on the tubular flowers, bulking up before winter dormancy.

description: Rhizomatous perennial ‘shrublet’ of moist oak woodland margins and seeps whose late-season wind-pollinated flowers still anchor a distinctive insect herbivore community on aromatic foliage. Tallamy-style keystone value is less about showy nectar and more about biomass: abundant soft leaves feed a wide guild of moth and butterfly larvae while dense stems provide cover for ground-foraging birds near water.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Natural along draws and seasonal channels; avoid planting in stagnant low spots unless you want aggressive spread
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Loamy to silty soils with reliable winter moisture but summer dry-down; tolerates a wide pH band where drainage is adequate

maintenance.wateringNotes: Calscape allows up to about monthly summer irrigation once established; more water increases spread by rhizomes. In naturalistic plantings, bias toward cool-season moisture and dry summer crowns unless you are managing a wet meadow aesthetic.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut rangy stems to near ground after dormancy; remove frost-burned tops
maintenance.pruningNotes: Winter cleanup reduces thatch and refreshes spring growth without removing autumn insect shelter if delayed until true dormancy.
maintenance.specialNotes: Can outcompete delicate neighbors in irrigated beds—use as a structural matrix along bioswales, not in tiny mixed borders.

seed.description: Light wind-dispersed achenes; local expansion is often vegetative via rhizomes rather than heavy seed rain

phenology.ecologicalValue: High leaf area for generalist herbivores; seasonal stems add vertical structure for dragonflies and songbirds near moist edges.

wildlife.notes: Vanessa cardui larvae are documented on many Asteraceae, including Artemisia, adding flexible host capacity in foothill gardens.
Uses dense herbaceous tangles along moist woodland edges for nesting cover and foraging lanes.
Papilio rutulus adults nectar at available late-season composites and streamside resources near mugwort stands.
Large native carabid predators hunt in moist litter and thatch layers maintained by mugwort’s spreading habit.

description: Resinous evergreen shrub of sun-baked chaparral and oak openings whose white-to-lavender flowers offer nectar during early summer dryness. It is not a classical keystone like oaks, but it is a Tallamy-relevant ‘tough-structure’ plant: sticky foliage reduces ungulate browsing while still supporting specialist pollinators adapted to glandular surfaces.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on rocky, well-drained slopes; intolerant of summer-wet clay bowls
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean mineral soils; tolerates drought; avoid rich compost pockets that hold moisture against the crown

maintenance.wateringNotes: No regular summer irrigation once established; occasional deep soak only the first cool season after planting on severe sites.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead twigs; thin interior only if airflow is poor
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal pruning preserves resinous foliage chemistry and spring flower buds formed on older wood.
maintenance.specialNotes: Rhizomatous spread can produce thickets—plan for footprint expansion on embankments.

seed.description: Small capsules release seed after midsummer bloom; germination often tied to open mineral soil between shrubs

phenology.ecologicalValue: Mid-summer nectar for bees and hummingbirds; evergreen leaves add vertical structure for chaparral birds.

wildlife.notes: Visits clustered bell-shaped flowers during the dry-season nectar gap in foothill openings.
Uses early-summer foothill flowers along woodland–chaparral ecotones.
Large carpenter bees rob nectar by slitting corolla bases on sticky, tubular flowers.
Uses dense evergreen shrubs for year-round cover adjacent to yerba santa thickets.

description: Sierra foothill endemic bush monkeyflower with large, showy cream-to-peach flowers that are magnets for hummingbirds and native bees. More localized than the broadly distributed D. aurantiacus, with 2× the observations in the Auburn bounding box (28 vs 14) — a true foothill specialist. Sticky foliage supports a specialized suite of insect herbivores adapted to resinous monkeyflowers.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Best on rocky banks, road cuts, and well-drained slopes; benefits from afternoon shade in the hottest Auburn sites
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Fast-draining loam to rocky soil; intolerant of heavy summer irrigation combined with poor drainage

maintenance.wateringNotes: Occasional deep summer water extends bloom in hot foothill gardens; avoid overhead sprinklers which promote foliar disease on sticky leaves.
maintenance.pruningTask: Pinch tips to encourage bushiness; remove winter-damaged stems
maintenance.pruningNotes: Early-spring shaping sets wood before the main bloom wave without removing all flower buds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Deadheading can extend bloom in garden settings; leave some seed heads if naturalizing on banks.

seed.description: Capsules release tiny seeds after midsummer; self-sows on open mineral soil

phenology.ecologicalValue: Long-season hummingbird nectar; sticky foliage supports specialized insect herbivores within the monkeyflower guild.

wildlife.notes: Primary visitor to large tubular monkeyflowers in Sierra foothill gardens.
Uses foothill flower corridors during migration and breeding where riparian edges meet dry slopes.
Euphydryas chalcedona larvae use bush monkeyflower foliage as a primary host in foothill habitats.
Forages monkeyflower tubes where corolla geometry permits legitimate entry.

description: The dominant foothill milkweed of the Sierra Nevada, with distinctive heart-shaped leaves clasping the stem and deep crimson-purple flower clusters. Far more abundant in the Auburn area than narrowleaf milkweed — this is the milkweed monarchs actually encounter in oak woodland and chaparral openings here. A keystone forb that hosts monarch larvae while supporting a specialized milkweed insect community.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Rocky slopes, road cuts, and chaparral openings; naturally grows on dry, well-drained hillsides
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained rocky or sandy loam; tolerates lean, dry soils; avoid heavy clay or wet winter conditions

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer irrigation once established — this is a true dryland milkweed adapted to Sierra foothill drought. Water only during the first establishment year.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove previous year's dead stems after monarchs have emerged
maintenance.pruningNotes: Delay cutting until late winter to protect overwintering invertebrates in hollow stems; new shoots emerge from the rootstock in spring.
maintenance.specialNotes: Expect aphids and milkweed bugs — they are part of a healthy milkweed patch. Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides near blooming plants.

seed.description: Large spindle-shaped follicles split to release silk-borne seeds on summer winds; seeds colonize rocky openings and road cuts

phenology.ecologicalValue: Monarch host plant; complex floral morphology supports diverse pollinators; milky latex structures a specialized arthropod community including milkweed bugs and beetles.

wildlife.notes: Danaus plexippus larvae feed on milkweed foliage; heart-leaf milkweed is the primary monarch host plant in Sierra foothill oak woodland.
Workers and queens forage on the deep crimson flower clusters; complex floral traps ensure effective pollination.
Specialist seed-feeder on milkweed follicles; a sign of a functioning milkweed patch, not a pest.
Pulls at silk and seeds as follicles mature, linking milkweed patches to finch energy budgets.

description: A delicate woodland iris of Sierra foothill oak openings and grassy slopes — one of the most frequently observed native plants in the Auburn area with 130+ research-grade observations. Its cream-to-purple flowers with intricate veining bloom in spring, providing early-season nectar when pollinators are emerging. Forms dense clumps that stabilize thin foothill soils.

plantingRequirements.sunExposure: Partial Shade, Full Sun (with afternoon shade in hot foothills)
plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Oak woodland edges, grassy slopes, and light gaps; does well on north-facing or east-facing slopes
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained loam to clay loam; tolerates seasonal dryness; prefers some organic matter from leaf litter

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer water once established — goes dormant in summer like most foothill bulbs and rhizomatous perennials. Let it rest dry.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dried foliage after summer dormancy begins if desired for aesthetics
maintenance.pruningNotes: Mostly maintenance-free; the clump expands slowly and rarely needs division.
maintenance.specialNotes: Deer-resistant. Combine with other shade-tolerant foothill perennials under oaks for a naturalistic woodland garden.

seed.description: Capsules mature after bloom; seeds drop near parent plants and are dispersed by gravity and ants

phenology.ecologicalValue: Early spring nectar and pollen for emerging native bees and butterflies; dense rhizomatous growth stabilizes foothill soils and provides ground-level cover for invertebrates.

wildlife.notes: Early-emerging queens forage on iris flowers during the critical spring build-up period.
Visits iris flowers for nectar, especially the deeper-tubed purple forms.
Spring migrants use iris nectar during the northward movement through foothill grasslands.

description: Silvery-leaved foothill lupine whose tall blue flower spikes fix nitrogen and feed long-tongued bees during the spring bloom arc. Tallamy framing treats many native lupines as keystone forbs because they combine Rhizobium-driven soil facilitation with pollen-heavy inflorescences that support diverse bee and butterfly visitation in lean soils.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Classic on dry banks and rocky openings; excellent erosion-control morphology once established
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy to rocky soils; avoid heavy compost that fuels rank, short-lived growth

maintenance.wateringNotes: Calscape emphasizes very low summer water; over-irrigation promotes root pathogens and collapses the silvery, drought-adapted habit.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove spent flower spikes after pods set if you want a tidier habit
maintenance.pruningNotes: Early-summer deadheading trades some self-seeding for cleaner structure; leave pods if expanding local populations is the goal.
maintenance.specialNotes: Nitrogen fixation benefits neighbors slowly—think decades-scale soil improvement, not instant fertilizer replacement.

seed.description: Hairy legume pods mature in early summer; seeds germinate after cool-season cues in open soil

phenology.ecologicalValue: Spring pollen and nectar pulses; seeds for finches; nitrogen facilitation in harsh mineral soils.

wildlife.notes: Glaucopsyche lygdamus uses lupines as primary hosts across much of its California range.
Visits lupine spikes where corolla geometry permits access in warm foothill gardens.
Buzz-pollinates papilionaceous flowers, improving outcrossing in patchy stands.
Extracts seeds from mature lupine pods in shrubby openings.

description: Tall pink-flowered annual of oak woodland edges whose late-season bloom fills the gap after spring ephemerals fade. Not a keystone in the oak sense, but Tallamy-relevant as a pulse forb that stitches together woodland pollinator networks with evening-flying hawkmoth pollination and lightweight cover for small insects.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Under high canopy or along north-facing woodland edges; avoid dense shade that prevents bloom
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy loam to loam; tolerates dry summer soils once plants are up—classic oak understory wildflower soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Fall-sown populations use winter rain; garden transplants may take light March–April irrigation to bridge dry springs, then taper to zero before bloom.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove browned annual stems after seeds shatter
maintenance.pruningNotes: Delay removal if you want self-sowing—shake ripe capsules over bare soil instead of sending them to green waste.
maintenance.specialNotes: Best treated as a self-reseeding annual mosaic under oaks rather than a manicured groundcover monoculture.

seed.description: Capsules mature midsummer into fall; seeds persist in soil until cool-season germination cues return

phenology.ecologicalValue: Late spring–summer nectar for moths and butterflies; fine-stemmed structure for small beneficial insects in partial shade.

wildlife.notes: Specialist hawkmoth named for its association with Clarkia flowers in California oak woodland systems.
Adults nectar along woodland edges where clarkias bloom under canyon live oak and foothill pine canopies.
Evening flights align with onagraceous flowers that open into dusk.
Moves through clarkia patches in shrubby oak understories while gleaning small insects from nearby foliage.

description: California’s iconic spring annual (often behaving as a short-lived perennial in mild microclimates) whose orange cups light up grassy oak openings. Tallamy context: modest specialist diversity compared with woody keystone plants, but very high landscape connectivity for generalist pollinators and finches on tiny seeds.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Road verges, meadow pockets, and sunny openings; intolerant of deep shade under dense canopy
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, fast-draining soils; tolerates poor foothill mineral soils where seed can contact mineral soil

maintenance.wateringNotes: Rainfall usually suffices; optional light March–April irrigation helps germination in unnaturally dry springs—avoid summer sprinklers that favor exotic weeds.
maintenance.pruningTask: Pull or cut browned plants after seed shed if aesthetics require
maintenance.pruningNotes: If self-sowing is desired, leave tan skeletons until capsules rattle.
maintenance.specialNotes: Reseeds best on disturbed mineral soil; thick mulch layers can suppress next year’s cohort.

seed.description: Elongate capsules split to release many small seeds as plants senesce into early summer heat

phenology.ecologicalValue: Spring pollen and nectar pulse; seeds for finches; bright color increases pollinator discovery in sparse early-season landscapes.

wildlife.notes: Hyles lineata adults visit open, bright flowers in spring wildflower patches across foothill grasslands.
Phyciodes mylitta adults nectar at spring composites and open Papaveraceae in sunny openings.
Feeds on small seeds in weedy–wildflower mosaics where poppies mix with native grasses.
Metallic halictid bee commonly filmed on bright spring flowers in California gardens.

description: Rhizomatous perennial ground layer with flat white inflorescences that pack hundreds of tiny disc florets attractive to short-tongued bees and many small butterflies. Tallamy framing: not a canopy keystone, but a workhorse forb that increases insect surface area at ground level and stabilizes soils on slopes.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Useful on gentle slopes for soil binding; spreads by rhizomes—plan containment if mixing with delicate forbs
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Tolerates a wide soil range if not constantly soggy; pH flexible in foothill gardens

maintenance.wateringNotes: Low water overall; occasional summer irrigation keeps foliage fresher in hot interior sites but is not required in naturalistic meadows.
maintenance.pruningTask: Shear spent flower heads; divide crowded patches if centers die out
maintenance.pruningNotes: Late-fall cutback reduces mildew carryover and refreshes spring basal growth.
maintenance.specialNotes: Can dominate small beds—pair with assertive neighbors or use in larger matrix plantings.

seed.description: Tiny cypselae mature after midsummer; local spread is often vegetative via rhizomes rather than heavy seed rain

phenology.ecologicalValue: Long-season small-flower nectar and pollen; dense foliage hosts aphid complexes that feed insectivorous birds when managed without sprays.

wildlife.notes: Phyciodes pulchella larvae use native Asteraceae including yarrows in montane and foothill populations.
Adults use flat-topped white inflorescences during migration pulses.
Shreds composite heads for small seeds in late-summer meadow edges.
Works dense disc florets efficiently, packing pollen on corbiculae.

description: The signature conifer of California's Sierra foothills — a graceful, open-crowned pine with blue-gray needles and massive cones that produce protein-rich seeds essential to woodpeckers, jays, and squirrels. A keystone genus (Pinus) with 169 local observations, making it the 8th most-observed native plant in the Auburn bounding box. Its open structure allows an oak-woodland understory to thrive beneath it, and its persistent dead branches provide critical cavity-nesting habitat.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Thrives on hot, dry, rocky slopes — the characteristic tree of Auburn's exposed foothill ridges
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained rocky or gravelly soils; tolerates serpentine and nutrient-poor substrates; intolerant of clay with poor drainage

maintenance.wateringNotes: No irrigation after establishment. Extremely drought-adapted — evolved on the driest, hottest sites in the Sierra foothills. Summer watering can actually promote root rot.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead lower branches for clearance if needed; thin crossing branches
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal pruning required. The open, airy crown is the natural growth form — do not try to make it dense. Remove only dead wood and branches that pose safety concerns. Large cones (up to 10 inches) can be heavy — consider placement away from walkways.
maintenance.specialNotes: Grows fast but develops an open, sculptural form. Large seed-bearing cones are heavy and can be a hazard near pathways. Plant with adequate space — mature trees reach 40-70 feet. The massive cones are themselves wildlife habitat.

seed.description: Massive cones (6-10 inches) ripen in fall, producing large, nutritious pine nuts highly sought by woodpeckers, jays, and squirrels

phenology.ecologicalValue: Large protein-rich seeds are a keystone food source for Acorn Woodpeckers, Steller's Jays, and Western Gray Squirrels. Dead branch cavities provide nesting habitat for owls and woodpeckers. Open crown allows light to reach understory plants.

wildlife.notes: Harvests and caches gray pine nuts in granary trees; one of the most important seed sources for foothill woodpecker colonies.
Cracks open large pine cones for nutritious seeds; caches surplus nuts for winter — inadvertently planting new pines.
Relies on gray pine nuts as a primary food source in foothill oak-pine woodland; also nests in the open canopy.
Nests in cavities of dead gray pine branches; forages bark for insects year-round.
